author: @dennisallen9135
text:
So, you made a very difficult task, easy.  Thank you.  I do have a couple questions, but I think I will watch the other videos first to see if you answer them.

Back with my questions and a comment.  I was first going to ask where do I measure the length from.  At first I was thinking the entire length of the wood.  Then I thought, ok to the rabbet.  Then was it the front edge or the back edge of the rabbet? Then my final thought was, to the front edge of the mount bracket to the end of the tongue.  Now my thought is, does it really matter, as long as they are all in scale.  The difference as you would say it, is a note or a sound.  If the note or the sound are in scale with each other you created a scale.  Then you can tune them to notes.  

So if I take off the wood of the tongue, lets say I take it off the end.  How much of a gap between tongues do I start effecting the sound box or echo chamber?  Yes I know I could take off the bottom, just thinking outside the box and wondering when the echo chamber starts effecting the other tongues as well.  Knowing that more vibrating air escaping at a different rate is going to have effects on the other tongues as well.  Or does it?  

What are the different effects if I were to make my rabbets wider or deeper?  If I made my Rabbet depth the set depth, but to tune to a note, if I took off a millimeter of the bottom or off the leading or back edge, of that rabbet, would there be a difference?  

OK, I know I could do these test myself, but I thought with your SIMPLE scientific approach and SIMPLE way of making me, an idiot, understand it, you could make others think outside the box even more.  

I am on my way to see what else I can learn from you.  Great job.

"she stands up from her seat and begins with a round of  👏, she motions a gesture from the bottom of her ❤ , 👏 BRAVO my Good 👍 man, Bravo." author: @fogdog165
text: Brilliant!  Except for the fact that the frequencies you used were based on "tempered" tuning which is necessary for multiple keys.  In this case you're only using one key so there's no advantage to using the tempered tuning system.  It will sound better and more natural if you use "mean" tuning.  Whatever key you're using the desired ratio of the eight frequencies would be 16:18:20:21:24:26:30:32.  The sizes of the tongues should be around the same ratios as well.

replies.author: @fogdog165
replies.text: Desmond Bagley Actually no, mean tuning refers to aligning everything to one note and as long as you play in only that key it sounds glorious but other keys, to various degrees, don't sound right.  With tempered tuning everything is slightly but equally out of tune relative to mean tuning and was adopted to allow for playing in more than one key.  Fretless stringed instruments and wind instruments allow the player to find the sweet spot for each note in any key whereas the piano or other essentially fixed tuning instruments the tempered tuning system is needed to allow for multiple keys.  The tongue drum will be in only one key so no tempering is needed, mean tuning is ideal for that instrument.
